text:
Modern medicine is woefully incomplete. It treats the body like a broken machine, and because it is embedded in a profit-driven system, it relies on quick fixes that manage symptoms instead of conditions that create health. The system doesn’t benefit from curing root causes, it benefits from repeat customers. 

But a body is not a machine. It's a dynamic system of chemistry, physics, emotion, memory, habit, and environment. Symptoms don't emerge in isolation.

Modern medicine excels at emergencies and fails at complexity. It can stop a heart attack but not explain the slow collapse of vitality we call "aging." It treats symptoms but usually doesn’t address root causes.

Somewhere along the way, doctors stopped asking, "Why?" But every symptom has a story. Every story has a pattern. And healing begins when someone finally asks, "What is this pattern trying to resolve?" 

I work in the territory that question belongs to. My practice has been mostly private so far...small, quiet, referral-only, but very effective. I’ve watched people who believed they were “untreatable” come back to life when someone finally looked at their system as a whole. 

I’ve seen people medicated for anxiety who actually had blood sugar instability. People labeled “depressed” when they were actually grieving (and mineral deficient). 

"Mystery cases” where no one had ever bothered to ask about micronutrients, hydration, poops, sensory stress load, or the emotional injuries they were carrying in their fascia and broader energetic field. No one had ever looked at their skin really closely, or their eyes, hair, nails, soles, tongues — basic diagnostic windows that reveal deficiencies and systemic stress.   

No one had paid attention to the way their breathing changed around certain topics, how their natural body odor smelled, the rhythm of their daily lives. 

Most doctors don't have the time, the patience, or the breadth of study to assess individuals as cohesive wholes. 

I’m not anti-medicine; I respect it deeply and was at one time pre-med myself. But modern medicine's paradigm is narrow by design: break reality into isolated parts, control the part, ignore the whole. 

Healing isn’t always linear, though, and bodies aren't just mechanical. Health doesn’t follow a single discipline. It encompasses bioelectricity, biochemistry, belief architecture, environment, and the meaning a person assigns to their own existence. 

If you don’t work at the intersection of (all) systems, you can't actually heal, you can only manage. True healing requires shifting conditions, not just suppressing signals. "Symptoms" are messages from our systems that something is wrong. 

People often assume that because I talk about energetics, subtle fields, and nonphysical mechanics that I must reject science. No. I reject bad science, lazy conclusions, and the arrogance that confuses incomplete measurement with impossibility. 

The nervous system is electric. Cells communicate through biofields. Emotion alters physiology. Trauma reshapes organ function. Belief rewires brain patterns. There's so, so much more to the bodies, and world, we inhabit than modern science yet understands. So much exists beyond the borders of ordinary sensory perception (with a wink to those with extraordinary abilities). And whether we use biomedical language or metaphysical language, we’re describing different layers of the same thing, anyway. 

I am a skeptic by nature and I love to test things. I notice patterns. I'm attuned to minute shifts and the variable changes which precede them. I derive immense pleasure from helping people and fixing things. 

Good medicine comes from the intersection of heart, hands, and mind, and I'm grateful to have all three at my disposal. The very best medicine restores agency through active patient participation, and I'm grateful also for everyone with whom I've had the privilege of working on healing so far. 

My work going forward, as I see it, is about communicating these things not as "alternative medicine," but as "complete medicine." The kind that restores sovereignty and teaches people to read their own systems. So, stay tuned for more of that.

text:
This image may look very woo, but it's actually a simplified diagram of the direct current potentials of the human nervous system.

The head and spinal region are strongly positive, with the three specific regions of greatest positive potential being the brain, the brachial plexus between the shoulder blades, and the lumbar enlargement at the base of the spinal cord. 

Potentials become increasingly negative out along nerve fibers, in the extremities.

text:
There is a lie stitched into some psyches so early and so repeatedly that it became a hidden law inside the nervous system:

“If I am fully myself, I will destroy what I love.”

They learned to hold back. Dim down. Contort. Scan the room for any potential consequences of their existence.

The lie makes them:

Over-responsible for others' emotional states.

Magnetized to the broken, the lost, and the exiled.

Brilliant at reading threat and reality distortion in others.

Afraid of their own magnitude.

This is why their hearts so often ache and their bodies stay braced: they are chronically compensating for fears that are not their own.

text:
There are two basic motivating forces: fear and love. When we are afraid, we pull back from life. When we are in love, we open to all that life has to offer with passion, excitement, and acceptance. We need to learn to love ourselves first, in all our glory and our imperfections. If we cannot love ourselves, we cannot fully open to our ability to love others or our potential to create. Evolution and all hopes for a better world rest in the fearlessness and open-hearted vision of people who embrace life.

John Lennon

text:
Some change I've made to my routines in the past month or so has dramatically changed my skin and hair. My skin is the softest it has ever been, softer than I even thought was possible, softer even than when I was steam rooming, exfoliating, and full-body moisturizing daily. And my hair! Silky and shining, from frizzy and dry.

I've made a number of changes, so I'm not sure if it's any single one or the conglomerate, but:

- eating ~200g of liver weekly
- 70-120g of protein daily
- 3-4 eggs daily
- 1.25mg oral minoxidil daily
- eliminating truly all of my known dietary allergens

text:
Have you noticed how the timeline pairs posts together, thematically? Now, consider: for posts to be paired and presented together, others had to make them - independently - for the algorithm to work with. And each post about X or Y topic thus increases the probability that others like it are also seen. And everyone who sees X or Y is that much more likely to remix and repeat the message - it's psychological priming, monkey see monkey do. 

Everything in the universe works this way. We co-create not only our own timelines, but also others' timelines, with the natural laws of echo and rhyme.

Each signal amplifies others like it and memetically self-propagates.

Ok 100g+ a day is actually not so easy and requires diligence and intention that I am not accustomed to involving in my eating habits. I do feel much better when I hit the 100g, though, and can tell a huge difference in my physique, energy, and stamina. Gonna def get some protein powder and incorporate gelatin; that should make it easier.

text:
People with unhealed trauma will often project the image of their abuser(s) onto others later in a subconscious attempt to rewrite the script in a way that makes them feel empowered. 

If someone relentlessly misconstrues your words, misinterprets your actions, incorrectly assumes your motives, twists the narrative to frame themselves as a victim, or even fabricates things outright, consider that they are likely in a deeply triggered and dysregulated state, unaware of what they're really doing. 

It isn't personal and there probably isn't anything you can do to "fix" it.

In this state, they're not capable of logical reasoning because their nervous system is firing as if you're their primary aggressor, and thoughts follow feelings without conscious awareness and intentionality. You can't talk them out of their very real feelings.

They're reliving old wounds, not present in the moment, and any attempt at intimacy, comfort, or repair is likely to feel like encroachment and control.

The best thing to do is stay calm yourself. Give them space. Remind them that you care. Tell them that they're warmly welcome back if/when they're ready (and you're truly open to that). And let it be.

They can't help being triggered and you can't help them out of it as long as their psyche is using you as a stand-in for the processing of repressed memories.

text:
When you’re entering a healthier relationship than you’re used to, you WILL get triggered. You WILL misread cues. You WILL overreact, shut down, cling, withdraw, snap, dissociate, or even ghost.

Your nervous system is adjusting to a new pattern it hasn’t learned yet.

This is normal, predictable, and fixable.

Regulate first, reflect later.

When you’re still activated, reflection often becomes self-punishment, so focus on returning to baseline first.

Practice slow exhale-focused breathing. Splash cool water on your face, wrists, neck. Sit or lay on the floor with your back supported. Go for a walk. Hum, sing, or talk out loud to stimulate the vagus nerve. Pay close attention to your senses: what you can see, hear, smell, taste, feel.

Once your system settles, then reflect.

Expect a shame spike. When you “behave badly,” your body floods with cortisol and adrenaline and your mind often goes to a threat-based self-assessment (“I messed up, I’m unsafe”). Old relational memories may resurface and try to convince you that things have always been this way and will always be this way, that all people are like this and you're better off alone.

This is just your system trying to protect itself and course-correct too hard, too fast.

You can interrupt this by naming it as a biological state, not a moral failing.

Your inner critic is a trauma-formed protector. It may try to protect you by telling you that you aren't capable of connection and don't want it, anyway. It may try to keep you from losing connection by shaming you into “fixing” yourself.

Common shame thoughts are like:

“They probably hate me now.”

“I’m too much.”

“I shouldn’t have reacted like that.”

“This proves I can’t do relationships.”

These thoughts are body-based alarms, not true statements.

Practice a neutral, curious debrief instead of self-attack and doomering.

Try asking yourself: 

“What was the trigger?”

“What did my body perceive as danger?”

“What need of mine was underneath the reaction?”

This shifts you from shame to understanding.

Healthy relationships are built through misattunement, repair, and re-attunement, not flawless behavior.

In fact, the ability to come back after a rupture is more predictive of relational security than avoiding rupture altogether.

Healthy partners don’t expect perfection. They expect honesty, repair, and steadiness over time.

Healthy repair sounds like:

“Hey, I got overwhelmed and overreacted. I’m sorry for that.”

“I wasn’t pulling away from you, I was dysregulated.”

“I want to show up with more clarity next time.”

Not:

“I’m such an idiot.”

“I ruin everything.”

Repair is about reconnection, not self-flagellation.

And remember to track your micro-wins!

Did you catch your overreaction sooner than last time? Did you name your trigger even once? Did you return to the conversation instead of spiraling?

These are signs of growing capacity, and even small steps can be huge when you're in uncharted territory like this.

If you’ve spent years, or even decades, in unsafe dynamics, your body isn’t going to instantly switch to secure mode. You’re literally rewiring your nervous system, and that takes time.

When you slip up, imagine how you’d speak to a child learning a new skill, or a friend trying something brave. 💛

This is normal, predictable, and fixable.

text: Sometimes I like going to the gym during busy times because it's so much. I put in one earbud for my own music while something else plays overhead. I can see the whole room reflected in the windows the elliptical faces, and the wall-length mirror to my left in the reflection as well as my peripheral vision, and the reflection through an interior window of the hallway (and the room reflected back in that glass) + the reflection of the hall's wall-length mirror perpendicular to the other, and through the reflection of another set of windows to the front door through which the foyer's motion-activated lights can be seen whenever anyone comes or goes, and (of course) the bustling street and sidewalk on the other side of the glass. Reflections in reflections and reflections through reflections. It satisfies whatever part of my brain feels the need to Keep Track of Everything and in an odd way I feel very relaxed.

text: I was scrolling one night a few years back, "looking for answers" as one sometimes does, and all of a sudden the page changed to that. Just that, like when you open an image in a new tab. I hadn't clicked anything, so...weird. I saved it because I thought it was cool and then hit the back button. I was returned to the feed, right where I'd been. No one had posted the image. And the forward button wasn't an option, as if I'd never even been on the page with the image. So I clicked through every profile for several posts up and down to see if anyone had it as a PFP or banner or posted at all and no one did.

text:
Do you know how a hologram works? A hologram doesn’t just store an image in one place, like a photograph or schematic; it distributes the whole across every fragment of itself. You can break it into infinitely small pieces and each piece will still contain the full pattern, encoded as interference.

Life works the same way. Every moment is a sliver of the total field, carrying the signature of the whole. And when you change anything...one belief, one expectation, one item placed here or there...you shift the interference pattern of the entire thing. Nothing is ever “too small” to matter. 🦋

text:
Heal, so you don't end up treating others the horrible ways you were treated. Because you will, if you don't heal, and you'll feel justified in it, too. You'll feel stressed, walled-in, attacked, and you'll do whatever feels most natural. You'll do whatever you learned people do when they're overwhelmed. Did they call you names? You'll call names. Did they yell? You'll yell. Did they make you feel irrelevant, powerless, afraid? Did they abandon you, attack you, neglect you? That's what you'll create for others unless you HEAL.

The people who treated you that way weren't doing it to be evil. They felt all the same sorts of things you feel, all the fear and anxiety and exhaustion, and that's how they handled it, because they learned it from someone before them and didn't heal.

Do the work they didn't. Rise above it all. Be better than they were. You can.

There weren't any blackeyed peas at the local grocery store, so I'm doing a mix of black and white beans and just hoping that balances out.

There also, predictably, weren't any collards, so kale chips it is.

There's a joke about trading luck and wealth for health in here. 😋

text:
Research has shown that positive thinking has a direct impact on brain function and structure, boosting serotonin, lowering cortisol, and activating dopamine neurons. This facilitates greater neural connectivity for enhanced adaptability, learning, and memory retention.

One  simple way to harness the power of positive thinking is with a gratitude  jar. Start with an empty jar, then at least once daily write a note  about something for which you are grateful. At the end of the year (or  whenever you need an extra boost along the way), you can take out all  your little notes and review how much you've had to be grateful for.

raw.full_text:
Research has shown that positive thinking has a direct impact on brain function and structure, boosting serotonin, lowering cortisol, and activating dopamine neurons. This facilitates greater neural connectivity for enhanced adaptability, learning, and memory retention.

One  simple way to harness the power of positive thinking is with a gratitude  jar. Start with an empty jar, then at least once daily write a note  about something for which you are grateful. At the end of the year (or  whenever you need an extra boost along the way), you can take out all  your little notes and review how much you've had to be grateful for.

text:
"Everyone gives what he has. The warrior gives strength, the merchant gives merchandise, the teacher teachings, the farmer rice, the fisher fish."

"Yes indeed. And what is it now what you've got to give? What is it that you've learned, what you're able to do?"

"I can think. I can wait. I can fast."

"That's everything?"

"I believe, that's everything!"

"And what's the use of that? For example, the fasting -- what is it good for?"

"It is very good, sir. When a person has nothing to eat, fasting is the smartest thing he could do. When, for example, Siddhartha hadn't learned to fast, he would have to accept any kind of service before this day is up, whether it may be with you or wherever, because hunger would force him to do so. But like this, Siddhartha can wait calmly, he knows no impatience, he knows no emergency, for a long time he can allow hunger to besiege him and can laugh about it. This, sir, is what fasting is good for."

Siddhartha, Herman Hesse

text:
The desire to "punish" another often stems from a deep subconscious need to resolve one's own trauma from being on the receiving end of "punishment."

Experiences in which one is victimized teach the subconscious that direct, good-faith communication is not possible, yet the need and drive to communicate remains, often siphoned through more symbolic avenues.

Tone, posture, narrative control. Life itself becomes the theater in which one subconsciously tries to make sense of and resolve past experiences by performing their inverses. The tormented becomes the tormentor to feel powerful, to feel safe.

text:
I don't block anyone unless they're being genuinely actively hateful/abusive. Being dumb, misguided, and wrong are fine. Even annoying is allowed. I try to keep in mind that I also have at times been dumb, misguided, wrong, and even annoying, and that we're all learning and growing. Blocking significantly impacts another account's reach  which is counter to learning and growth through engagement.

The one exception is whenever I come across a random account that has me blocked. I don't come here and hit anyone with negativity; I'm rarely ever even rude or sarcastic. So when I see that someone has taken that kind of swing at this account, I go ahead and block them with all of mine. If you're gonna hit my reach like that without cause I'm gonna hit yours back even harder.

text: I am literally the only person on the planet with my name. I am the only person who has ever had my name in all of history. Both my first and last name are unusual and almost invariably mispronounced. This gave me some anxiety as a child sometimes, sure, because I was shy - but then, it also gave me an arena in which to explore my relationship with my self-confidence and relation to others. I learned to have humility, security, and humor about others making such "mistakes." And I didn't have *anyone* else to attach my identity to on the basis of name kinship, which undoubtedly fostered a sense of individuality. I was in one elementary school class where three girls all had the same first name and had to be differentiated with last initials; even then I remember feeling sorry for them in their lack of individuation. And now, as an adult, having a one-of-a-kind name has been great for personal branding (search results will invariably be about me) and integrity (no plausible deniability or sense of smallness/anonymity for anything and everything I do which could in any way be tied to my name). So I'm firmly team unique name, but then again it probably also has a lot to do with individual temperament.

text:
Reached out to a couple former schoolmates at a top company, one significantly higher up the ladder than the other, with the same question.

The higher up one responded warmly within literally 30 minutes.

The lower down one didn't open my message until the next day and then left me on read.

Lesson in there.

text: A key difference between Finnish and American culture that I've observed is a Finnish objection to the concept of getting paid to do anything, like the concept of receiving payment or "earning more" itself is somehow offensive. Whereas, in America we like to offer payment for services pretty freely, and entrepreneurship, "side-gigging" etc. are generally well-looked upon through an American lens. "Earning money" is good. "Hiring help" is bougie. Receiving services from someone without paying them feels exploitative.

"Hiring help" is bougie. Receiving services from someone without paying them feels exploitative.

text:
John McCarthy famously wrote in his proposal for the first conference on artifical intelligence at Dartmouth College,

"The study is to proceed on the basis of the conjecture that every aspect of learning or any other feature of intelligence can in principle be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it."

This can furthermore be shortened to read simply, 

"Everything can in principle be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it."

And prompts the question: at what point is a "simulation" no longer merely a simulation?

Grok helped me fix it, thank you Grok!

Everything that didn't work: 

Restarting app
Force stopping and restarting app
Clearing app cache
Clearing app data
Restarting phone 
Reinstalling app
Restarting phone after reinstall 
APKMirror from January 
APKMirror from December 
Accessing via Firefox mobile
" requesting desktop version 
New tab
Restarting Firefox
Switching from mobile data to wifi
Updating System Webview 
Turning off ETP

What finally worked:
Clearing duolingo[dot]com cookies and site data in Firefox 

🥴

text:
I've been looking for four-leaf clovers lately on my morning walks and not finding any, and feeling kind of down about it.

So yesterday, or the day before, I said out loud in my head, directionally to who- or what-ever might be able to have some sway in helping me to see some, "I promise that if I get to see any I won't pick a single one, I will just admire them and leave them be," (in different words). 

Still, I didn't see any. For a day, or two. And I felt disappointed, but kept my promise in mind, and kept keeping an eye out.

I've seen so many things: various types of bottle caps, mostly metal ones - a couple wrapped in foil; cigarette butter, but more than those, halfsies, or even nearly fullsies; various little flowers and native herbs; a young anole; empty water bottles; a few good sticks; various types of rock of various sizes, red clay, and broken brick; BMW, Maserati, Volvo; a broken vape pen slowly crushed to pieces in a parking lot over the course of several day; a very large G; sundry species' scat; the silhouette of an unfamiliar bird, very smooth outline; and many other such precious and beautiful things. Not very many other people.

Until today.

Today, a friendly man and his friendly dog right out of the gate. Then, a couple fellas hanging out at the park. THEN (omg another one!), a kinda weird looking dude shuffling down the middle of the road. 

I had meant to walk that way, but decided I would just as rather avoid the foreseeable there and turned around instead, and walked about ten steps, and there on my left was a four-leaf clover. And something funny happened in my brain, because it registered in my field of vision...all at once...not just one or two, but...focus...count em...one (yksi), two (kaksi), three (kolme), four (nelja) ... viisi.

raw.full_text:
I've been looking for four-leaf clovers lately on my morning walks and not finding any, and feeling kind of down about it.

So yesterday, or the day before, I said out loud in my head, directionally to who- or what-ever might be able to have some sway in helping me to see some, "I promise that if I get to see any I won't pick a single one, I will just admire them and leave them be," (in different words). 

Still, I didn't see any. For a day, or two. And I felt disappointed, but kept my promise in mind, and kept keeping an eye out.

I've seen so many things: various types of bottle caps, mostly metal ones - a couple wrapped in foil; cigarette butter, but more than those, halfsies, or even nearly fullsies; various little flowers and native herbs; a young anole; empty water bottles; a few good sticks; various types of rock of various sizes, red clay, and broken brick; BMW, Maserati, Volvo; a broken vape pen slowly crushed to pieces in a parking lot over the course of several day; a very large G; sundry species' scat; the silhouette of an unfamiliar bird, very smooth outline; and many other such precious and beautiful things. Not very many other people.

Until today.

Today, a friendly man and his friendly dog right out of the gate. Then, a couple fellas hanging out at the park. THEN (omg another one!), a kinda weird looking dude shuffling down the middle of the road. 

I had meant to walk that way, but decided I would just as rather avoid the foreseeable there and turned around instead, and walked about ten steps, and there on my left was a four-leaf clover. And something funny happened in my brain, because it registered in my field of vision...all at once...not just one or two, but...focus...count em...one (yksi), two (kaksi), three (kolme), four (nelja) ... viisi.

text:
Every time I share a nude (or even just implied) here for years now, an adult content warning is added to my account and my reach - even to followers - is harshly limited. The limiting is harsher each time. After the last photo, I was allotted 2 views in 5 minutes on an innocent text post. I understand that not everyone wants to see "adult content," but some people do, and to the extent that kind of content is truly allowed here, it should also be given due reach, and if the content can be flagged automatically it can just as easily be filtered out only for those who don't wish to see it. 

This account may end up reachnuked to oblivion, we're already mostly there honestly, but I won't stop posting, on principle. Censorship is lame. Second, this is my art, and I want to share it. I enjoy sharing it, and other people enjoy seeing it. Third (probably should be first), nudity and indeed even SEX are NATURAL and OK, even GOOD. How is babbey fourmed? Don't we want moar babbey? Like, come on.
